findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as PROBLEM
findings.suggestion: You've got the problem statement buried in your startup information slide—it needs its own dedicated slide early in the deck, right after your cover. Pull out the core pain points (delayed lead responses, low conversion rates from poor qualification, lack of immersive viewing options, agent burnout) and give each one a concrete number or example from the UAE market, like "Foreign investors wait 48+ hours for agent responses" or "Traditional brokers convert only 15% of qualified leads." This makes the problem visceral and sets up why MIRA's solution matters before you dive into features.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as SOLUTION
findings.suggestion: You have your solution buried in Slide 2 as part of a longer "Startup Information" section, but investors need it as its own dedicated slide that visually shows how MIRA works end-to-end. Create a standalone Solution slide that maps the six-step customer journey from your Technology Overview (Discovery → Matching & Presentation → Valuation & Advisory → Virtual Exploration → Conversion & Closing → After-Sales Support) as a visual flow, with one or two key features highlighted per stage—this makes the complexity of what you're building immediately clear and memorable, rather than asking investors to extract it from dense paragraphs.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 8, Slide 9, Slide 10

findings.label: Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as FINANCIALS
findings.suggestion: You have AED 800K in revenue and AED 50M in transaction volume, but investors can't see the unit economics that prove this is actually working—add a simple one-page financials slide showing monthly revenue trend (last 6 months), gross margin by revenue stream (commission vs. SaaS vs. ancillary), and your cash burn rate so they understand if you're on track to profitability or how long your runway is. For example: "Month 1-3: AED 150K revenue, 65% gross margin from commissions | Month 4-6: AED 250K revenue, 72% gross margin" plus "Monthly burn: AED 400

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 6

findings.label: Does the deck include a Market slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as MARKET
findings.suggestion: You need to add a dedicated market slide that quantifies the UAE and MENA real estate market size, growth rate, and your specific addressable market within it. Right now you're burying critical market context in your investment rationale slide—add one slide showing the total market (e.g., "UAE real estate transactions: $X billion annually"), your serviceable addressable market (SAM) based on the segments you're targeting like off-plan properties and foreign investors, and how your AED 50M in six months of traction positions you relative to that opportunity. This gives investors immediate clarity on whether you're chasing a $10B or $100B problem.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as COMPETITION
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that maps MIRA against 3-4 specific competitors operating in UAE/MENA real estate—name them (Property Finder, Bayut, Huspy, Dubizzle), show side-by-side how MIRA's AI-first approach, commission structure, and property matching differ, and explicitly state what defensible advantage MIRA has (e.g., "Only platform with live ROI forecasting + AR visualization + 24/7 AI agent" or "Proprietary DLD integration that competitors lack"). This directly addresses the investor concern flagged in your diligence report about unclear differentiation and first-mover advantage.

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
findings.evidence: Deck has 11 slides (minimum 8)

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: LLM returned non-JSON after retry
findings.suggestion: You're listing AR/VR + NLP + CRM as your tech stack, but investors will immediately ask whether you built the AI model yourself or wrapped third-party LLMs—and that answer determines your defensibility story. Add one concrete technical detail: either explain your proprietary approach to emotional intelligence in buyer conversations (what makes your NLP layer different from ChatGPT), or be transparent that you're using OpenAI/Claude and show your moat instead through your real estate data integrations, DLD API access, or your conversion rate validation (the 70% number is strong—tie it back to what your tech actually does better).

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: unclear if MIRA has strong defensible IP or first-mover advantage
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that maps out your specific IP assets and switching costs. For example: "Proprietary emotional intelligence layer trained on 50,000+ UAE property transactions (patent pending), exclusive data partnerships with DLD and three major developers that competitors cannot easily replicate, and 18-month integration lock-in through custom CRM workflows." Right now you're claiming "deep integration" but not explaining why a buyer or developer couldn't switch to Property Finder or Bayut if they built similar features—spell out what makes leaving MIRA painful or impossible.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: What is the current development status of MIRA's AI/AR platform? Is the technology live, beta, or roadmap?
findings.suggestion: Add a single, clear line to your Technology slide stating exactly where MIRA stands: "Platform Status: Live in UAE pilot with 3 enterprise customers (Q4 2024–present)" or whatever your actual stage is. Right now you're claiming "strong traction and revenue" but investors can't tell if you're running a proof-of-concept with one broker or a scaled operation—that ambiguity makes them doubt the metrics, not trust them.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Can you provide demonstrations or live access to the full customer journey, showcasing all claimed functionalities?
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide that walks through a real customer interaction step-by-step: buyer logs in → AI agent asks about budget and preferences → AR visualization loads a property → ROI forecast is generated → buyer books a viewing. Show actual screenshots or a 2-minute video walkthrough of this flow working end-to-end, because right now investors see your 70% conversion rate and AR/VR claims but have no proof the platform actually delivers them together in practice.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Is the AI model proprietary or built using third-party LLMs? Is there any IP protection?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated section on your tech stack that explicitly states whether your AI model is proprietary, fine-tuned, or built on third-party LLMs like OpenAI/Claude, and what IP you own versus license. For example: "MIRA's NLP engine is built on [LLM provider], fine-tuned on 50K+ UAE property transactions with proprietary training data; we hold patents on [specific AR visualization method] and exclusive licensing agreements with [partners]." This directly answers the investor question flagged in your diligence and removes the ambiguity that's currently feeding competitive and defensibility concerns.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: How is data privacy and security ensured, especially for high net worth and institutional clients?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated security & compliance section that spells out exactly how you protect high-net-worth client data—for example: "All client financial profiles and transaction data encrypted end-to-end using AES-256; SOC 2 Type II certification completed Q3 2024; compliance with UAE DLD data residency requirements and GDPR for UK/EU clients; annual third-party penetration testing by [firm name]." This directly answers the investor question and shows you've thought through the real risks your HNW clients care about.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Please provide CAC vs. LTV data per customer type (B2B and B2C).
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing your actual unit economics split by B2B (developer partners) and B2C (individual buyers): for each segment, show how much you spend to acquire one customer (CAC), what lifetime value they generate (LTV), and your payback period — for example, "B2B Developer CAC: AED 5K, LTV: AED 180K, Payback: 4 months" versus "B2C Buyer CAC: AED 800, LTV: AED 12K, Payback: 18 months." This directly answers investor questions about unit economics and shows you understand which customer type actually drives your profitability.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: What proportion of claimed AED800K revenue is recurring, and what is derived from one-off deals?
findings.suggestion: Break down your AED800K revenue into two clear numbers: how much is recurring monthly/annual contracts (the stuff that comes back every month) versus one-time project fees or pilot payments, then show the trend — for example, "AED320K recurring (40%) from 8 signed developer partnerships at AED40K/year each, AED480K one-time (60%) from initial implementations." This tells investors which revenue actually sticks around and scales.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: What is your go-to-market strategy in neCw geographies (e.g., expansion to Saudi Arabia, UK)? How do you plan to acquire customers in these markets – organic vs. paid strategies? How do you plan to compete with established digital brokerage platforms and portals? Is there an internal sales team oOr partner-led channel strategy?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing questions investors have rather than answering them — flip this to show your actual plan. For Saudi Arabia specifically, write something like: "Partner with Emaar and Damac (who already use MIRA in UAE) to co-market to their Saudi developments, targeting 40% of customer acquisition through these existing relationships in year one, with paid search handling the remaining 60% focused on expat investor keywords." This shows you know your channels, have leverage with real partners, and have thought through the unit economics of each.

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple growth chart to your traction slide showing your revenue progression month-by-month for the last 6-12 months, with the actual AED figures labeled on each data point — for example, if you went from AED 50K in month one to AED 800K now, show that trajectory with the percentage growth rate called out (like "16x growth in 11 months"). This gives investors an immediate visual of momentum and makes your AED 800K claim feel real, not just a number.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: Please provide customer feedback, case studies, or NPS scores from early adopters.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing 2-3 specific customer wins with their logos, deal size, and one concrete outcome — for example: "Emaar Properties: 450 qualified leads in first 90 days" or "Damac Developers: 32% conversion lift on property inquiries." This transforms your AED800K revenue claim from a number into proof that real, recognizable players trust MIRA enough to pay for it.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong domain expertise across deal advisory, fundraising, and governance, but you're missing a dedicated technology leader on your core team—someone who owns the product and platform development for your ecosystem hub. Add a CTO or VP of Engineering to your leadership roster with specific experience building marketplace or community platforms, so investors see you can actually execute the tech infrastructure that connects all these stakeholders.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now your team slide lists expertise areas but doesn't show why these specific people are credible—investors need to see what each founder has actually built or led before. Add one concrete credential per core founder (CEO, CTO, etc.) like "Founded XYZ startup that reached $5M ARR" or "Led deal advisory at [firm] closing 50+ transactions" directly under their name, so investors immediately understand the relevant experience that qualifies them to run this business.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual experts listed, but investors need to see who's actually leading your company—add a dedicated "Leadership Team" section with your CEO, co-founders, and key executives at the top, including their relevant background (e.g., "Founder & CEO: [Name], former [relevant role] at [credible company]"). Your advisors are valuable, but they work best when positioned below a clear internal leadership structure that shows who's running the ship day-to-day.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple one-liner to your team slide stating the founder equity split and vesting schedule—something like "Founders: 60% equity vesting over 4 years with 1-year cliff" positioned near your founder names. Investors need to see this upfront to understand governance and commitment, and it signals you've thought through cap table structure from day one.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service. Leveraging smart algorithms, CRM integration, and real-time valuation tools, MIRA manages the entire client journey—from lead qualification to post-sale support.
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide on regulatory compliance and licensing that spells out exactly which UAE real estate and AI regulations MIRA operates under, what licenses you currently hold (or are pursuing), and how you've structured the platform to comply with DLD rules around virtual agents and data handling—for example, "MIRA operates under UAE Real Estate Regulatory Agency guidelines and holds [specific license type]; our AI agent architecture complies with [specific regulation] by [concrete implementation detail]." This removes a major investor concern flagged in your own diligence report and shows you've thought through the compliance moat, not just the tech moat.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide addressing your legal and regulatory standing—specifically confirm whether MIRA holds all required real estate brokerage licenses in the UAE, has resolved any compliance issues with DLD or financial regulators, and clarify your data handling practices for international client information. For example: "Regulatory Status: MIRA operates under UAE Real Estate Regulatory Authority License #[X], complies with DFSA data privacy standards, and has completed DLD integration audits as of [date]—no pending disputes or regulatory actions." This removes a major diligence blocker early and signals you've thought through the compliance landscape seriously.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Good news—your numbers are internally consistent across the slides, so you don't have a math problem to fix. However, you're missing a critical bridge: the AED 800K revenue you claim in six months doesn't clearly connect to your six revenue streams, so investors can't see which channels are actually working. Add a simple breakdown showing what portion of that AED 800K came from commissions vs. developer partnerships vs. ancillary services—even rough percentages will make your traction credible and help investors understand which business model to bet on.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service... This automation and intelligence is intended to disrupt local and global real estate markets by delivering scalable, data-driven services that traditional agents cannot match

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo, hllooerggeyyin AA, inns nninuuteaanll dRReeedpp sooolrrettly
findings.suggestion: You've got corrupted text scattered throughout your deck—that garbled string "TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo..." appears repeatedly in footers and disclaimers, and there are random character breaks like "devDelopment," "exp licitly," "neCw," and "oOr" that make you look careless. Do a full find-and-replace pass to clean up every instance of that corrupted footer text, fix the broken words, and ensure consistent spacing and formatting across all slides—investors notice these details and they undermine your credibility on a serious $2.2M ask.
